title: Somalia's Constitutional Clock Reaches Critical Stage: April 14 Parliamentary Mandate Expires in 5 Days — No Agreement
detail: As of April 9, 2026, Somalia stands five days from the April 14 expiry of the Federal Parliament's mandate under the 2012 Provisional Constitution, with no negotiated electoral agreement reached between the Federal Government of Somalia (FGS) and the opposition 'Council for the Future of Somalia' (comprising Puntland, Jubbaland, opposition parties, and civil society). The constitutional crisis has become the dominant story in Somali politics, with the Royal United Services Institute (RUSI) publishing an analysis titled 'Crunch Time in Somalia' and the International Crisis Group warning of severe instability. Key fault lines as of April 9: (1) The FGS maintains that the March 2026 constitutional amendments — which extended parliamentary terms to five years — are valid, pushing the next election to May 2027; (2) The opposition argues the amendment lacked a quorum (161/275 House members, 34/54 senators voted, short of 184 and 36 required) and that the parliament's mandate expires April 14 regardless; (3) Puntland has withdrawn recognition of the FGS since March 31 and is operating independently; (4) Jubbaland is aligned with the opposition position; (5) Southwest State is under FGS control following the March 30 military seizure of Baidoa. The constitutional impasse risks creating a governance vacuum that Al-Shabaab could exploit — diverting both security attention and public legitimacy from the counter-insurgency campaign. The UN Security Council is expected to discuss Somalia in its April session, and UNSOM Special Representative Catriona Laing has called for dialogue. With parliament unable or unwilling to convene for an electoral committee, international election observers and the AU have expressed serious concern.

media.caption: Somalia's Constitutional Clock Reaches Critical Stage: April 14 Parliamentary Mandate Expires in 5 Days — No Agreement
